title: Taylor Approximation of Inventory Policies for One-Warehouse, Multi-Retailer Systems with Demand Feature Information
abstract: We consider a distribution system in which retailers replenish perishable goods from a warehouse, which, in turn, replenishes from an outside source. Demand at each retailer depends on exogenous features and a random shock, and unfulfilled demand is lost. The objective is to obtain a data-driven replenishment and allocation policy that minimizes the average inventory cost per time period. The extant data-driven methods either cannot guarantee a feasible solution for out-of-sample feature observations or generate one with excessive computational time. We propose a policy that resolves these issues in two steps. In the first step, we assume that the distributions of features and random shocks are known. We develop an effective heuristic policy by using Taylor expansion to approximate the retailer’s inventory cost. The resulting solution is closed-form, referred to asTaylor Approximation(TA) policy. We show that the TA policy is asymptotically optimal in the number of retailers. In the second step, we apply the linear quantile regression and kernel density estimation to the TA solution to obtain the data-driven policy calledData-Driven Taylor Approximation(DDTA) policy. We prove that the DDTA policy is consistent with the TA policy. A numerical study shows that the DDTA policy is very effective. Using a real data set provided by Fresh Hema, we show that the DDTA policy reduces the average cost by 11.0% compared with Hema’s policy. Finally, we show that the main results still hold in the cases of correlated demand features, positive lead times, and censored demand.

article.1. Introduction: Fresh Hema, a subsidiary of Alibaba Group, is a grocery chain that owns more than 200 stores and offers fresh goods to customers in more than 20 major cities in China as of 2021. The focal logistics system that motivates this research is a central warehouse located in Chengdu city which is responsible for inventory replenishment for 16 retail stores scattered in the city. The company adopts a centralized inventory system in which the warehouse orders fresh goods, such as vegetables, eggs, and meat, from local farmers every morning. The ordered products usually arrive at the warehouse in the early morning of the following day and then are immediately loaded onto trucks that deliver the products to the retail store by 6:00 a.m. on the same day. The fresh goods market is very competitive in China because there are many traditional, local fresh goods markets in high-population areas. Fresh goods usually have very short shelf lives; therefore, the company requires an accurate demand estimation for inventory replenishment to stay competitive. For this reason, the company has started to collect feature information for retail stores such as past sales, demographics, and page views in recent years. The feature information plays a key role in the inventory decision as it can help predict the next day’s demand for the retail store. For example, when the company observes a larger volume of page views for an item, the demand for the item for the following day is often higher. Nevertheless, the company is seeking a simple and implementable inventory policy that utilizes the feature data. This is the question that we aim to address in this paper. There is a long history in the inventory literature of the distribution system without demand feature information. It is known that the optimal policy for the general lead-time model with backorders, if it existed, would be very difficult to characterize. Nevertheless,Clark and Scarf (1960)show that, under the so-called balance assumption, the myopic allocation is optimal and the optimal policy can be obtained as follows. First, each retailer computes its optimal base-stock level as if it has ample supply from the warehouse. Then, an induced penalty cost is charged to the warehouse for not being able to allocate enough inventory to reach the optimal base-stock level of the retailer. Finally, the warehouse solves a constrained optimization problem by minimizing the sum of its holding cost and the induced penalty costs to determine the optimal order quantity and allocation policy. If our model does not consider the feature information, the model satisfies the balance assumption because excess inventory is disposed of and unfilled demand is lost. The optimal policy turns out to be quite simple: the warehouse orders an amount equal to the sum of the optimal order quantities of retailers and ships each retailer the ordered quantity after it arrives. (See theNo-Feature(NF) model inSection 3.) That is, there is no allocation decision to make, and the warehouse becomes a cross-docking location. In fact, Fresh Hema adopts this idea to implement its inventory policy. Specifically, Hema uses machine learning methods to estimate the demand function of features based on historical data. At the beginning of the day, the retailer observes the features, calculates the order quantity based on the demand function according to the newsvendor solution, and places an order with the warehouse, which ships the ordered inventory to the retailer the following day. In other words, Hema adopts cross-docking and usespastfeature information for inventory replenishment. Although Hema’s approach is easy to use, one natural question is whether there exists a data-driven policy that outperforms Hema’s current practice. Intuitively, the warehouse can better allocate inventory available according to more accurate demand information after thecurrentfeatures are observed. As shown in this paper, the allocation decision plays a critical role in the current model when the feature information is present. We consider a one-warehouse, multiretailer system in which a central warehouse orders from an outside unlimited source and allocates inventory toJnonidentical retailers which face random demands. There is one period of lead time for the warehouse and zero lead time for the retailers, that is, same-day delivery. (A general lead-time case is discussed inSection 6.) Demand for each retailerjis correlated with exogenous features and a random shock. Unfulfilled demand is lost and excess inventory is disposed of. There are linear holding costs and lost-sales costs evaluated at the end of a period. An inventory planner places an order for the warehouse at the beginning of a period. The order arrives the following day, and an allocation decision is made after observing the random demand features. The objective is to obtain a data-driven, feature-dependent replenishment and allocation policy such that the long-run average cost per period is minimized. Using the extant data-driven methods in our model to provide the inventory solution (prescriptive predictor) for given features encounters challenges. Specifically, one may use the empirical risk minimization (ERM) approach (e.g.,Ban and Rudin 2019) to train the inventory solution by solving the simple average cost ofN-constrained optimization problems whereNis the in-sample data size. However, the computational time is extensive ifJandNare large. In addition, the resulting solution may not satisfy the allocation constraint for any out-of-sample feature observations. Alternatively, one may adopt the local learning (LL) framework ofBertsimas and Kallus (2020), which uses in-sample data to train the weight functions for given features. Although this approach can generate a feasible solution, like ERM, the LL approach also needs to solveN-constrained optimization problems in the training step, which incurs significant computational time. (Please refer toSection 7for a numerical study.) We propose a two-step procedure to tackle these challenges and generate a simple and implementable data-driven policy. The key idea is to integrate the allocation decision into the inventory solution through Taylor expansion. Specifically, in the first step, we assume distributions are known and approximate the retailers’ cost functions by Taylor expansion. Solving the problem with the approximated cost functions, we can derive a closed-form solution for the order and allocation decisions for the warehouse. We refer to the resulting heuristic as theTaylor Approximation(TA) policy. The required components to execute the TA policy are the retailers’ base-stock levels and the density for given features at the base-stock level. Thus, in the second step, we apply machine learning methods to estimate these required components. Specifically, we use linear quantile regression to generate the retailers’ base-stock levels and kernel density estimation to generate the density. We refer to the resulting data-driven policy as theData-Driven Taylor Approximation(DDTA) policy. This work made the following contributions. First, with the known demand distributions, we show that the TA policy is asymptotically optimal in the number of retailers (Theorem 1). This result is established by showing that the difference between the total cost of the TA policy and that of alower-boundsystem is bounded by a constant. The lower-bound system is constructed by assuming that the demand feature information is known in advance when the warehouse replenishes inventory at the beginning of a period. (See theAdvanced Feature(AF) model inSection 3.) With this assumption, the warehouse knows precisely the retailers’ order quantities generated by the assumed known feature information so that the resulting cost is a lower bound to the optimal cost. Second, we extend the data-driven policy with feature information from single-stage systems to distribution systems. The DDTA policy originates from the TA policy that bypasses extensive computations on the inventory allocation decision. We show the convergence rate for the estimation error on the total cost of the DDTA policy to that of the TA policy (Theorem 2). We also derive the generalization bound, that is, the difference between the generalization error (or the out-of-sample cost) and the empirical in-sample cost, for the DDTA policy (Theorem 3). CombiningTheorems 1–3, we show the effectiveness of our simple data-driven policy. We believe that such a two-step framework, that is, obtaining a simple solution to ensure feasibility and effectiveness and applying machine learning methods to the simple solution, can be applied to other complex constrained optimization problems. Finally, we conduct a case study by applying our DDTA policy to a real data set provided by Fresh Hema. We compare our policy with Fresh Hema’s current policy and find that the DDTA policy can generate a solution in less than a second and outperform Hema’s policy by 11.0%. The rest of the paper is organized as follows.Section 2reviews the literature.Section 3introduces the model.Section 4derives the TA policy when the demand and feature distributions are known.Section 5applies data-driven methods based on the TA policy to generate the DDTA policy.Section 6considers several extensions by incorporating correlated demand features, general lead times, and censored demands.Section 7numerically tests the effectiveness of the DDTA policy compared with other data-driven policies.Section 8conducts a case study based on Fresh Hema’s data.Section 9concludes the paper. Online Appendix A presents the proofs for our main results. Online Appendix B provides discussions, proofs, and the details omitted in the paper.
article.2. Literature Review: We study a distribution system with nonidentical retailers whose demands are correlated with exogenous features. There are two closely related streams of literature: distribution systems with known demand distribution and data-driven inventory models. The distribution inventory system with known demand distribution has been studied extensively since the seminal work ofClark and Scarf (1960). It is well known that, under the so-called balance assumption, that is, that inventory among retailers can be transferred instantaneously and costlessly, the optimal joint replenishment and allocation policy is that the warehouse orders up to an echelon base-stock level and allocates its inventory according to the myopic allocation rule. Nevertheless, without the balance assumption, the optimal policy remains unknown. Thus, there is a stream of research aiming to propose simple and effective heuristic policies or derive lower bounds to the optimal cost, for example,Nahmias and Schmidt (1984),Jackson (1988),Chen and Zheng (1994),Axsäter et al. (2002), andGallego et al. (2007). A paper more relevant to our model isMarklund and Rosling (2012), who consider a one-warehouse–multiretailer system in a finite horizon. They propose an approximate model by revising the allocation constraint in the Clark-Scarf model. Specifically, the authors constrain the sum of expected inventory positions and show that the resulting cost is a lower bound to the optimal one. There are many variants to the classic Clark-Scarf model. For example,Perakis et al. (2020)consider inventory allocation of multiple products across a network of warehouses with both the inventory depth and capacity constraints. They propose an iterative algorithm that converges to the optimal solution.Govindarajan et al. (2021)consider a multilocation newsvendor network when only the mean vector and covariance of the joint distribution of demands are known. They adopt a distributionally robust model to find inventory levels that minimize the worst-case expected cost.Zhu et al. (2021)evaluate a class of easy-to-implement heuristics, called modified echelon(𝑟,𝑄)(r,Q)policies, and show that the proposed policy is asymptotically optimal, as pairs of system primitives, such as the ratios of the fixed cost of the central facility to those of the local facilities, are scaled up.Miao et al. (2022)consider an allocation problem for a fixed amount of inventory in a finite-time horizon. They show that the allocation policy based on Lagrangian relaxation is asymptotically optimal when the numbers of retailers and time periods approach infinity.Nambiar et al. (2021)study a similar problem and propose a computationally tractable algorithm under demand learning by using a Lagrangian relaxation approach. We refer the reader toSimchi-Levi and Zhao (2012)for a survey on the classic multiechelon inventory models. Our contribution to this stream of literature is that we propose a closed-form replenishment and allocation policy based on Taylor expansion, and show that the heuristic is asymptotically optimal. Note thatMarklund and Rosling (2012)andMiao et al. (2022)have derived similar asymptotic results with different approaches, but they do not consider demand with feature information. The literature on data-driven applications for inventory problems mainly focuses on newsvendor or single-stage models. This literature can be further categorized into whether there are feature data for the demand model. For the model without feature information,Liyanage and Shanthikumar (2005)consider a newsvendor model. Assuming the sample data are generated from a family of demand distributions, they derive the optimal order function for given data observations and optimize the parameters of the order function to derive the so-called “operational statistics” as the data-driven inventory solution.Levi et al. (2007)obtain the data-driven newsvendor solution based on sample average approximation (SAA). They derive the performance bound with respect to the sample size, and laterLevi et al. (2015)improve the finite sample bound.Huh et al. (2011)consider a newsvendor model with censored demands. They use the Kaplan-Meier estimator to derive an adaptive data-driven policy and show that the policy converges to the optimal one with the known demand distribution.Zhang et al. (2019)consider the same model ofHuh et al. (2011)and propose a new learning algorithm that has a square-root convergence rate that matches the theoretical bound.Chen and Chao (2020)study the optimal learning algorithms under substitution for multiple products with censored demand. This stream of research recently has been extended to nonstationary systems.Keskin et al. (2021)study a newsvendor model with unknown, time-varying demands over a finite horizon. They develop a nonparametric dynamic ordering policy with a time-moving window, which tracks the changes in the demand rate over time. For the model with demand feature information,Ban and Rudin (2019)consider a newsvendor model and assume that the optimal replenishment policy is a linear function of features. They apply ERM and kernel-weight optimization to generate the order quantity and show the value of feature information as well as the performance bounds on the out-of-sample costs.Bertsimas and Kallus (2020)propose a general framework for using data to prescribe optimal decisions. Their idea is to use nonparametric machine learning algorithms, such as k-nearest neighbor (kNN) and regression trees, to find the weight function based on features and use it to solve the weighted average of the objective value for out-of-sample data. They show the data-driven solution is asymptotically optimal when the sample size is large.Oroojlooyjadid et al. (2020)combine the ideas in deep learning and use the deep neural network to approximate decision function.Qi et al. (2020)extend the model inBan and Rudin (2019)by considering nonstationary and intertemporal demand and feature data with an unbounded domain.Bertsimas and Koduri (2021)further propose two methods based on regression in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces for solving the optimization problem with feature data. They refer to their methods as “global” machine learning methods as opposed to “local” ones inBertsimas and Kallus (2020), which fit a functional form of the prediction model rather than approximate objective functions based on local points near the observations. They provide theoretical guarantees for both methods under general demand functions.Elmachtoub and Grigas (2022)provide a “smart” predict-then-optimize (SPO) framework by introducing the SPO loss function which measures the cost of predicting error in the decision for an optimization problem.Kannan et al. (2022)study the data-driven SAA problem with covariates by the “predict-then-smart-optimize” (PTSO) framework. Another stream of data-driven inventory research adopts a distributionally robust optimization framework (DD-DRO) and minimizes the worst-case expected cost over some specifically chosen distribution ambiguity set. We refer toRahimian and Mehrotra (2019)for a detailed discussion.Qi et al. (2022)focus on a similar model with the “fixed design” features instead of the “random design.”Zhang et al. (2023)study a distributionally robust feature-based newsvendor problem and provide a class of optimal and interpretable policies with finite sample guarantees. The data-driven research for the multilocation inventory problem is relatively sparse.Acimovic and Graves (2015)study a fulfillment problem and develop a heuristic that minimizes the immediate outbound shipping cost and an estimate of future expected outbound shipping costs. They use a company’s data to estimate the required parameters in their heuristic. The general framework inBertsimas and Kallus (2020)can be used to solve the inventory allocation problem for the multiretailer model with demand features. However, as stated earlier, their algorithm requires extensive computations because of solving the allocation problem for each in-sample data observation during training.Notz and Pibernik (2022)study a capacity planning and allocation problem in a finite horizon. They formulate the problem into a two-stage optimization problem with recourse and propose two machine learning algorithms with out-of-sample guarantees based on sample average approximation and kernelized empirical risk minimization. However, their objective function is quite different from ours. Our contribution to this stream of research is that we extend the data-driven inventory policy to the distribution system with demand features.

For𝐿>0L>0, the model degenerates to the no-feature model described inSection 3, with the order and allocation decisions being the minimizers of (8), as the realized features do not affect the allocation decision because of feature independence. Note that the feature information can improve the order and allocation decisions for the general lead-time model when the features are correlated between times. This is essentially an extension ofSection 6.1with general lead times. Similar to the results inSection 6.1, we can show parallel results toTheorems 1–3. Retailer-Correlated Demand Features The problem becomes more involved when demand features are correlated between retailers. Unlike the time-correlated case, it is challenging to incorporate the feature correlation into the inventory order and allocation decisions. Thus, we only consider implementing the TA and DDTA policies under the retailer-correlated features. We show that the TA and DDTA policies possess similar properties as those inTheorems 1–3when the features between retailers follow a mixing process; that is, the correlation between retailerjand𝑗+𝑘j+ktends to zero when𝑘→∞k→∞; a numerical study also suggests the effectiveness of the DDTA policy in this case; see Online Appendix B.3 for detailed analysis and results. Nevertheless, we note that assuming a mixing process for retailer-correlated features is less practical. It remains an open question to design an effective data-driven inventory policy utilizing the feature correlation.
article.7. In the next section, we will focus on the performance of data-driven policies in Hema’s case study. Hema has more than 400 “daily fresh” products, and fast computation is an important criterion to achieve same-day delivery after observing the demand features. Even for a problem of moderate size (𝑁=400N=400,𝐽=16J=16), it requires around an hour to generate the solutions under LL or PTSO policies, which makes these policies hard to implement in practice. In contrast, the DDTA policy is more practical with still relatively good performance, which makes it more attractive to Hema.
article.8. Case Study: Fresh Hema: We first describe the data set and then select features from the feature set offered by the company. The data we present here are shown on relative scales to keep the original data set confidential. 8.1. Data Description The internal company data we collected consist of 6 months of daily inventory, sales, and feature records from October 2020 to April 2021 across the distribution system in a major city in China with 16 retail stores. We choose Chinese cabbage, labeled by Fresh Hema as “Daily Fresh,” as the considered inventory item to manage. Chinese cabbage has one day of shelf life and will be disposed of at the end of each day if not sold. Hema’s staff also keeps track of the in-stock period daily. With the sales amount in the in-stock period, Hema can calculate the hourly demand rate. Then, the lost sales can be estimated as the hourly demand rate times the out-of-stock period. By this procedure, Hema recovers the demand data from the censored sales data. SeeFigure 2for an example of sales and demand data for Chinese cabbage at a retail store. We shall use the recovered demand data as input for the case study here. 8.2. Feature Selection As Hema has listed more than 50 demand features in its original data set, we have to choose some of the most relevant ones in order to simplify our model. This is also recognized as the “data sparsity” issue in data-driven operation management problems; seeFeng and Shanthikumar (2022)for detailed discussions. Here, we simply apply the stepwise regression used widely in statistics to select significant covariates by the Akaike information criterion (AIC). We select four features, that is, historical sales before 7 days, number of Hema members, item page views, and number of items in the shopping cart, and𝑃=4P=4. We further conduct a hypothesis test on the data correlation, and it shows that each feature can be modeled by anARIMA⁡(𝑝,𝑑,𝑞)ARIMA⁡(p,d,q)process with i.i.d. random noise. Ad-order differencing method is applied to the feature sequence with𝑑>0d>0so that the feature data follow a stationaryARMA⁡(𝑝,𝑞)ARMA⁡(p,q)process, which satisfies bothAssumptions 5and6inSection 6.1and the mixing process in Assumption B8 in Online Appendix B.2; see Online Appendix B.7 for details. 8.3. Performance Comparison As stated inSection 6.1, although we can implement theDDTATCDDTATCpolicy for Hema’s data set, the policy is more involved and difficult to use in practice. We, therefore, focus on the DDTA policy. Another reason for considering the DDTA policy is that we can fairly compare its performance with the other data-driven policies that do not utilize the feature correlation information: the SAA policy, the LL policy byBertsimas and Kallus (2020), the PTSO policy inspired byKannan et al. (2022), and Hema’s replenishment policy (seeSection 1, the second paragraph, for the description). In other words, we are implementing these policies as if the feature data were generated from i.i.d. processes, although they are time correlated. Hema has an accounting department to estimate the cost parameters. For the considered item in this study, Hema estimates that a single unit of waste would costℎ′𝑗=3.5hj′=3.5and the lost-sales penalty𝑏𝑗=30bj=30is nearly 10 times larger for all𝑗∈[𝐽]+j∈[J]+. The waste cost at the warehouse isℎ0=2.5h0=2.5because there are no transshipment and disposal costs. All monetary values are in Renminbi (RMB), the currency of China. We use Hema’s data from October 2020 to February 2021 to train the above four policies and use the March 2021 data as a testing set. For the LL policy, we use kNN weights and set𝑘=13k=13, which is determined by the 10-fold cross-validation technique. We find that SAA performs poorly as the resulting cost is 89.0% higher than Hema’s current policy. This demonstrates the importance of using feature information in the replenishment decision. On the other hand, LL, PTSO, and DDTA outperform the current Hema policy. Specifically, compared with Hema’s policy, LL can lower the cost by 8.2%, PTSO can lower the cost by 12.0%, and DDTA can lower the cost by 11.0%. Both LL and PTSO require more than 130 seconds of total computation time, compared with DDTA, which requires only less than 0.5 seconds for the 31 instances in the test data set. Figure 3shows the average demand among all 16 retailers and corresponding allocation quantities under the DDTA policy and Hema’s policy. (Most retailers also follow the same pattern as in the figure.) It is clear that the allocation quantities under the DDTA policy follow the demand pattern better than Hema’s current policy. This is because (i) the allocation decision of the DDTA policy can reduce the order variability of the warehouse, and (ii) the DDTA policy uses the current feature information for inventory allocation whereas Hema’s policy uses the day-ahead feature information for inventory replenishment, resulting in a higher variance in demand shock.
article.9. Concluding Remarks: This paper proposes a data-driven inventory replenishment and allocation policy for a one-warehouse, multiretailer distribution system in which the demand for the retailer depends on features. The policy is derived from two steps. The first step is to assume distribution information is known and use Taylor expansion to approximate the retailer’s cost function. We derive a closed-form solution, referred to as the TA policy, by solving the approximated problem. We show the TA policy is asymptotically optimal in the number of retailers. The second step is to use machine learning algorithms to generate components that require demand distributions in the TA policy based on the historical demand and feature data. We show that the resulting DDTA policy is consistent with the TA policy. Our result suggests that when the size of the sample data and the number of retailers are sufficiently large, the DDTA policy is effective. We also use a real data set obtained from Fresh Hema to compare the DDTA policy with the current one. We show that the DDTA policy can reduce the cost by 11.0% for the considered item. Finally, we extend our basic model to the settings of correlated demand features, general lead times, and censored demand data. Our proposed policy is still proven to be efficient. There are open questions that are important and interesting for future study. First, our model considers a perishable, lost-sales distribution system with one period of lead time at the warehouse. One important research direction is to extend the current analysis to a nonperishable distribution system with backlogging. In fact, to our knowledge, the results for the backlogged system with general lead time and demand features are very limited even for single-stage models. It would be valuable to extend the analysis to provide simple and effective heuristic policies. Second, we currently study an offline problem and it would be interesting to explore how our data-driven Taylor-based heuristic could be applied to the online settings and its corresponding performance. 1Nonrandom features such as the day of the week, price, etc., can also be incorporated into our model.
